title: Part 9
text: members of the social group; and (4) reintegration of the disturbed social group or social recognition of an irreparable breach or schism (Turner 1957a: 91–4). 1957: A Pivotal Year It is not so difficult to establish when Turner’s work took a decisively new direction: this happened in 1957, the same year as his thesis was published. Turner was one of the most promising young anthropologists of his generation. He was sponsored by the established elite, trained in the canon, and his dissertation was well-received. Had he gone down the road prepared for him, we would still today know him as an important member of the British school of anthropology. Luckily, he just could not go down that road. 1957 was the pivotal year, but Turner was of course ‘prepared’ for the rupture that ensued. The notion of ‘social drama’ had already opened the door for another way of approaching social life. From his mother, Turner had inherited a profound interest in the theatrical and creative side of life. He had a lifelong interest not only in ritual, but also in art, literature and poetry (which he also wrote). There is also no doubt that Turner was transformed by the ethnographic context in which he found himself. Among the Ndembu rituals were omnipresent, and social life largely organized around a variety of such rituals. In her memory of their fieldwork periods, Edith Turner (1985: 2–3) tells how the beating of the ritual drums could be heard so often that she and Victor were simply forced by circumstance to rethink the social role of rituals. Almost in the secret, 4 and against 4 As argued also in Deflem’s reconstruction, Turner’s hesitation with regard to immersing himself fully in the Ndembu ritual complex was surely related to his position within the Rhodes-Livingstone Institute of Sociological Research (Kuper 1983:128–9, 150–53; Deflem 1991). Studies of ritual had a very low priority at the Institute, which focused on political and legal systems, urbanization, labour migration, and social and economic organization. The Institute produced studies with a high degree of (neo-Marxist) uniformity whereby ‘deviants and turncoats were treated with great ferocity internally, but no criticism was tolerated from outsiders’ (Kuper 1983:129). Liminality and the Modern 78 the explicit recommendations of Max Gluckman (who ordered Turner to cover every aspect of ‘social structure’ before engaging with the ritual ‘superstructure’), Turner slowly came to consider ritual the core of his interest (for further detail, see Deflem’s excellent discussion of Turner’s intellectual development, 1991). The year 1957 is pivotal for the intellectual history of liminality, starting with the obvious but important observation that this was the year Arnold van Gennep died. Unsurprisingly, the Année Sociologique made no mention of the fact in its subsequent issues. The year 1958 was also the 100th anniversary of Durkheim’s birth, and the front cover of the 1958 issue bore his image, almost as a symbol of van Gennep’s non-existence. Van Gennep died as he had for many decades been living: silently and without public attention. He was mourned only by a small circle of folklorists who knew about his enormous project, and with whom he had been collaborating. However, as if by destiny, something else happened in 1957, and it concerned the life and thought of Victor Turner. Turner published Schism and Continuity, introducing the notion of social drama. But what is less known is that in 1957 Turner also wrote his first essay on ritual proper (Turner 1957b). With this paper, not published until 1964 (it was circulated among a smaller group of friends), Turner to a large extent laid the foundation of his approach to ritual and started to mark his difference to the conflict theory framework of the Manchester School. As always with Victor Turner, academic matters did not evolve in a vacuum: they concerned a worldview, and ethos, and also, as Weber would call it, a conduct of life. In 1957 Turner resigned from the British Communist Party and renounced Marxism, and he would never turn back on his decision. Deflem (1991) suggests that this categorical break might also have been related to the Soviet suppression of the Hungarian uprising, an event that stirred heated debates in all European left- wing environments. Later that year Turner was received into the Roman Catholic Church. He remained a Catholic for the rest of his life. During his years as Simon Research Fellow, lecturer and senior lecturer at Victoria University of Manchester (1957 to 1963), Turner started to devote his time almost entirely to writings on Ndembu ritual. It was not going to go down well with his surroundings. In 1963 Turner was offered a professorship of anthropology at Cornell University in Ithaca, New York. He had previously been appointed a fellow at the Center for Advanced Studies in the Behavioral Sciences at Palo Alto, California (1961–1962). He accepted the offer and decided to move to America. British academia had become a straightjacket to Turner, and the experimental tendencies in American science would attract him much more (and here again the parallels to Gregory Bateson are more than obvious). 1963: Turner Encounters Arnold van Gennep With the reorientation towards process and ritual, moving outside Durkheimian functionalism and neo-Marxism, Turner seemed destined to encounter the work of Arnold van Gennep, but the precise manner in which it happened still merits our Liminality Rediscovered: With Victor Turner and Beyond 79 attention. Victor Turner, like all other Anglo-American anthropologists, did not really know about the work of Arnold van Gennep. During his fieldwork, Turner had read about van Gennep via the work of Henri Junod (Turner 1985: 159), so an indirect connection had been made. Luckily for the history of social thought, Solon Kimball had the felicitous intuition to translate Rites of Passage into English (otherwise we cannot be certain that Turner would actually have read it; he was not fluent in French). Turner stumbled upon van Gennep’s Rites of Passage almost by chance during the summer of 1963. Quite importantly, this happened at a moment when Turner was himself in a liminal state. He had already resigned from Manchester and also sold his house, but he was still waiting for his US visa which was continuously delayed because of his refusal of armed military service during WWII. The Turners were staying at Hastings on the English Channel, living in ‘a state of suspense’ (E. Turner 1985: 7). Turner literally lived at a spatio-temporal threshold when he encountered van Gennep. In contrast to Lévi-Strauss and his British followers, Turner experientially recognized the importance of van Gennep’s insight. The reading inspired him, on the spot, sitting at a small desk in the library at Hastings, to write the essay Betwixt and Between: The Liminal Period in Rites of Passage, the famous chapter in his 1967 publication, The Forest of Symbols. Turner presented the paper once in America, in March 1964, when he had finally taken up his position at Cornell. This would be the first of his explorations into liminality. The reading of van Gennep’s book became a touchstone for everything Turner did, said and thought after 1963. From what can be deduced from Turner’s own references, he did not delve deeper into van Gennep’s larger project. Rites of Passage is practically the only one of van Gennep’s works that Turner ever quoted. The book evidently offered Turner what he was in search of. Van Gennep’s analytical schema further helped Turner to redirect his work beyond the functionalist paradigm. In his analysis of Ndembu ritual, Turner (1967, 1969, 1974) showed how ritual passages served as moments of creativity that freshened up the societal make-up, and argued, against Durkheim (and therefore also against Radcliffe-Brown), that rituals were much more than mere reflections or elaborations of ‘social order’. Van Gennep’s framework and the concept of liminality complemented the term already introduced by Turner: ‘social drama’. Liminality and Ritual Passages among the Ndembu: The Development of Turner’s Approach Turner realized not only that he could adopt van Gennep’s analytical framework; like so many ethnographers who engaged with van Gennep, he recognized that van Gennep’s scheme was inherent in the ritual structure itself, and this was so even with respect to Ndembu emic terms that corresponded in detail to van Gennep’s etic ones. All the Ndembu rituals are characterized by the three-phased processual form of rites of passage (Turner 1967: 13–14): (1) separation (Ilembi or Kulemba), the treatment and dance to make the subjects sacred; (2) margin, a Liminality and the Modern 80 period of seclusion involving partial or complete separation of the subjects from everyday existence; and (3) re-aggregation (Ku-tumbuka), a further treatment and dance celebrating the end of the seclusion period. Van Gennep’s framework brought order into Turner’s data, but it also inspired him to dig deeper. Turner would increasingly insist on the ritual enactment of symbolic orders. He very much followed van Gennep in starting out by stressing the real spatial settings in which ritual transformation took place. This included a focus on the integrative aspects of ritual, but equally on its differentiating ones, such as the distinctions of gender and age groups. The religious component in ritual was now essential for Turner, and he would refuse to treat it like an epiphenomenon. How directly this relates to his own religious conversion is difficult to assess, but there can be little doubt that it had a role to play; or, perhaps rather, it all happened as part of one singular process that involved Turner’s life-work. In his work on the Chihamba ritual, for instance, Turner refused to explain away the religious aspect in ritual: ‘One has to consider religious phenomena in terms of religious ideas and doctrines’ (Turner 1975: 195), almost turning on its head Durkheim’s dictum that social phenomena need social explanation. For Turner this also implied a necessary attention paid to actual religious experience. For Turner ritual is religious, and religion involves both social experiences in concrete ritualistic activity and a systematic corpus of beliefs ‘which have for their object invisible and intangible beings or powers which a human group recognizes as superior, on which it depends’ (V. Turner and E. Turner 1982: 201). In a deeply reflexive vein, Turner (1975: 31–2) went further: ‘After many years as an agnostic and monistic materialist I learned from the Ndembu that ritual and its symbolism are not merely epiphenomena or disguises of deeper social and psychological processes, but have ontological value’. The Later Victor Turner In Turner’s later works (1982, 1985, 1988) a series of further developments took place. It is impossible to talk about any sudden shifts or reorientations. Rather, it seems evident that Turner slowly but surely became more and more interested in the universalistic elements of his approach. He also opened up new empirical fields of interest, including religious pilgrimage and carnivals in ‘complex societies’. His focus remained on ‘social dramas’ and liminal experience, but during the 1970s his fields of reference extended to include literature, film, images and various forms of spectacle. Perhaps most famously, in New York Turner came into contact with theatre and performance groups; this interaction led to an entire Turner-inspired school of performance studies, still today represented by one of Turner’s students there, Richard Schechner. Emblematically, one of Turner’s last books, published after his death, was entitled, From Ndembu to Broadway. The title in a way sums up the travels of the concept of liminality, but it also sums up Turner’s own trajectory. The Turners also went to Brazil, which led to an instant Liminality Rediscovered: With Victor Turner and Beyond 81 analysis of carnival and the Brazilian Umbanda ritual, which, as Turner noted, shared many parallels with Ndembu ritual, and some of them certainly as direct borrowings from African ritual. Finally, Turner took an interest in pilgrimage, again based on a participatory experience with his wife, Edith. Turner does not mention it, but pilgrimage had in fact been singled out by Arnold van Gennep as a particular form of ritual passage, and with specific reference to Catholic pilgrims (van Gennep 1960: 184–5). In the very last period of his life, before dying from a sudden heart attack in 1983, Turner had started to wonder about the connections between ‘body, brain and culture’ and seemed attracted toward cognitive science, which he was trying to combine with an evolutionary approach, sketched in the chapter Body, Brain and Culture. There might be potential value to such a direction, but since it is not the line of investigation we here wish to pursue, let us only briefly mention what Turner was concerned with here. Turner was convinced that ritual does not only ‘condense cherished socio-cultural values’, but that ‘through its liminal process, it holds the generating source of culture and structure’. He also found that this view stood in contradiction to established ethological views, where ritual behaviour is seen as ‘genetically programmed’. Turner’s interest in evolution and socio-biology was new. It was probably one more reason why he started to refer more and more to the work of Bateson, who likewise had argued that ‘play’ must perform a central role in evolution, since it is in play that humans and animals learn to communicate ‘as if’, e.g. to create frames that render symbols meaningful. 5 It is clear that ritual is enacted differently in various species, and Richard Schechner summed up these evolutionary differences very well in his preface to Turner’s last book, The Anthropology of Performance (Schechner 1988). This is a view that arguably complements the work of Gregory Bateson. However, Turner’s hope that research into the human brain would solve some of the mysteries he was struggling with is one that quite frankly must be considered a dead-end. Other developments took place in Turner’s late work that merit more of our attention, for it was these that started to indicate the applicability of liminality in ‘modernity’. Turner on the Edge of Anthropology: Liminality in Modernity The travels on which liminality went outside the study of ritual passages in small- scale societies, and in a way outside anthropology proper, began in Victor Turner’s own work. In his ethnographic accounts, Turner repeatedly identified parallels with non-tribal or ‘modern’ societies, clearly sensing that what he argued for the Ndembu had relevance far beyond the specific ethnographic context, but without unfolding any systematic analysis or comparison. He became more explicit about 5 Even this notion of ‘as if’, of life in the conditional, had to some extent been anticipated by van Gennep. On van Gennep’s notion of the ‘world-as-if’, see Belmont 1979: 96–7). Liminality and the Modern 82 such links toward the end of his life; and yet, it is precisely this part of Turner’s work that is in need of analytical elaboration. The question that kept puzzling Turner was a simple one. He had seen how decisive ritual passages and liminal experiences were for the Ndembu; and quite clearly, the same seemed to hold true among practically any ‘non-modern’ society. Ritual passages and liminal experiences gave form and rhythm to social groups, and also formed and shaped individual personalities, in ways that were certainly ‘structured’ but at the same time never perfectly pre-determined. Turner came to see liminality as the key to culture. The question was this: what happened to liminality within a horizon of the modern? Did it get lost? Did it survive? Did it mutate into something else? If so, then what? At the level of empirical application, Turner made two concrete suggestions: a. In a famous article, Liminal to Liminoid, in Play, Flow and Ritual: An Essay in Comparative Symbology (1982[1974]), Turner suggested that liminal experiences in modern consumerist societies to a large extent have been replaced by ‘liminoid’ moments, where creativity and uncertainty unfold in art and leisure activities. b. In his work on the Christian pilgrimage (1978), Turner argued that pilgrimage shares aspects of liminality because participants become equal, as they distance themselves from mundane structures and their social identities, leading to a homogenization of status and a strong sense of communitas. The suggestions proposed in From Liminal to Liminoid had huge effects on anthropology, as several of Turner’s students would draw inspiration from Turner and study art, theatre, literature and leisure. Turner argued that ‘traditional’ rites of passage had lost much of their importance in modern societies, but that they had been replaced to a large extent by other more mundane out-of-the-ordinary experiences that he termed liminoid. In art and leisure we recreate ‘life in the conditional’ and thus revive the playful; we shall have a chance to discuss this term in more detail in Chapter 7. For obvious reasons, Turner’s ideas have also become a reference point within literature studies, theatre and art. The idea of the liminoid was in many ways what made Turner really famous. While recognizing the importance of Victor Turner’s insights, we should hesitate to simply follow him (and his students) here. In this vein Turner’s much less discussed work on pilgrimage may in fact blaze the trail for us in a much more meaningful direction for further theorization. Turner here caught a crucial mechanism involved in the liminal process: the temporal and spatial fixation of liminal conditions. In his work on pilgrimage, Turner saw how a liminal state may become ‘fixed’, referring to a situation in which the suspended character of social life takes on a more permanent character (Turner 1978). This was much in line with what Turner himself had suggested earlier, namely that in the monastic and mendicant forms of the world religions, Liminality Rediscovered: With Victor Turner and Beyond 83 transition had become a permanent condition (1969: 107). We shall return to this ‘institutionalization of liminality’ in the chapters making up Part II, as Turner here came near to anticipating Arpad Szakolczai’s notion of ‘permanent liminality’ as diagnosing the modern. Beyond Victor Turner There is no doubt that any thinking with liminality in the current context must take its departure from Turner’s reading of van Gennep. However, it is equally important to point out a series of problems with the way in which Turner developed the concept of liminality within a horizon of modernity. First of all, Turner’s understanding of the liminal as relating in modern society primarily to art and leisure, sidelines some of the clearly dangerous or problematic aspects of liminality. To have a reading experience, to go to the theatre, or to enjoy an extraordinary holiday is all very good and certainly something of importance for both individuals and the larger consumer societies in which we live. But such experiences share very little of that danger and real peril involved in entering a liminal phase. Turner’s notion of the liminoid completely sidelines the extremely problematic nature of liminal experiences. Liminality involves a destruction of previous norms, but in ritual passages such destruction and a sudden void in values and social positions is tied together with a reformation of values and norms that are made public during the reintegration rituals; without such balancing acts of destruction and redress, liminality becomes pure danger. In terms of reception and development of Turner’s work and the concept of liminality, it is certainly not irrelevant that Turner’s ideas first started to spread around the late 1960s in America, a period so heavily marked by a taste for transgression and the celebration of out- of-the-ordinary experiences to break with everything ‘traditional’. Turner’s 1967 and 1969 publications gave instant fame to the term liminality, but the term was all too easily associated with 1968, the happy celebration of ‘difference’ and anti- structure. Turner’s ideas found even more fertile terrain with the postmodernist turn of the 1980s. Turner became a celebrated reference point in the 1980s and 1990s, as anthropology went through a ‘performative turn’ with a focus on process. ‘Process’ and ‘performance’ were always crucial terms to Turner. Here again the dangerous, troubling, anxiety-generating aspects of uncertain periods of transition, conflict, and crisis were simply ignored. Turner’s (albeit hesitant) self- identification with the postmodernist turn certainly opened up space for a usage of the term that he would, or should, have warned against. Second, and relatedly, by over-romanticizing the liminal experience, Turner’s account of the formation of communitas leaves something to be desired. Turner’s work may be read (indeed, has often been read) as a plea for people to engage uncritically in communitas-inspired action, defying the social order by inverting, or even perverting, its structural demands – as if any kind of destruction is useful or normatively sound. As amply indicated in the Ndembu ethnography (and the Liminality and the Modern 84 entire comparative record of threshold experiences in liminal contexts), the human emotions that are built up in the liminal period are actually perplexing and deeply ambivalent. As we know from military training, if orchestrated well, it is entirely possible to build up a blood-thirsty crowd of hungry young men who will kill whomever they meet on their way. In other words, it may well be that we should consider the very opposite relationship to that presented by Turner: that it is within the ‘structure’ and the everyday routine that our humanness and open attitude towards the ‘other’ resides, whereas the communitas built up in the liminal period may involve exactly that ‘undifferentiation’ and de-humanizing aspect which we at times may seem to feel as overburdening our normality. In liminality we may refresh our ethics; but in liminality the very moral foundation of sociability can also be broken down, and with devastating consequences. The communitas that comes out of liminality may be recognized as a deeply bonded human collectivity; but whether this collectivity engages in loving care toward the other or in violent destruction we really cannot to say in any general way. At a large-scale level, one can here think of Girard’s Kierkegaard-inspired discussion of the anonymous, ‘persecuting crowd’, to be discussed below. On this point, Gluckman’s critique of Turner is not simply to be dismissed. Gluckman argued that Turner’s distinction between structure and anti-structure is too rigid, and that communitas is significant only ‘within an established structure which is asserted again afterwards, and which indeed is asserted during the liminal period itself, by inversion’ (Gluckman and Gluckman 1977: 242). It is precisely this ‘assertion’ of the communitas spirit into everyday structure that is far from always unproblematic. Third, in his discussion of the liminoid, Turner seems to suggest an oversimplified dichotomy between symbolic systems of a ‘traditional’ and a ‘modern’ type, arguing that ‘we must distinguish between symbolic systems and genres which developed before and after the Industrial Revolution’ (1982: 30). This dichotomy seems overdrawn, and is arguably misleading. It also seems to stand at odds with what Turner indicates elsewhere, namely that the modern period takes shape around the early sixteenth century. In fact, it was during the early sixteenth century that modern theatre was invented (Szakolczai 2013a; see also Hastrup 2004 for an anthropological discussion of Shakespeare’s theatre), long before what we normally identify as the industrial revolution. It was of course also during the sixteenth century that mass production and consumption of books and pamphlets started to spread, with the invention of the printing press; that games and gambling started to proliferate, and so on. Fourth, and as discussed in greater detail in Chapter 7, in contrast to liminal experiences, liminoid experiences are optional. As individuals we simply choose when and how to ‘have an experience’. This therefore does not involve a resolution of a personal crisis or a change of status, nor does it engage the larger society in any encompassing way. The liminoid is a break from normality, a playful as-if experience, but as such it loses the very key feature of liminality, as argued by van Gennep: transition. The act of suspension is decoupled from real transformative processes. Of course, a reading experience or an art performance might, in very Liminality Rediscovered: With Victor Turner and Beyond 85 rare instances, 6 change our lives, how we see ourselves and the world around us; but most often it does not. To intoxicate oneself (however we may wish to do so) now and again does not necessarily serve as a question mark to normality; often quite the contrary. Fifth, by thus delimiting liminality, Turner underplayed the extent to which liminal moments or liminal experiences might be equally present in political or social transformations, i.e. outside ‘culture’, in the narrower understanding of that term. Here Turner was certainly influenced by the Parsonian version of anthropology as dealing with ‘symbol systems’ or ‘comparative symbology’. In a general sense, Turner was drawn towards performance genres and literature, and had relatively little interaction with political scientists. Despite Turner’s frequent references to ‘complex society’, and his many allusions to the relevance of liminality for ‘macropolitics’ (1988: 91), and despite his earlier engagement with political anthropology (see Swartz, Turner and Tuden 1966) his work remained largely a-political in character. The consequence is that we today know of Victor Turner if we study theatre and art; we know practically nothing of Turner if we study politics or sociology. 7 And that is not such an entirely obvious division of labour, given the potential of the liminality concept and its centrality for understanding transition and change. Given the very complex history of the reception of both Arnold van Gennep’s and Victor Turner’s works, let us be quite explicit about how, from our point of view, we ought not engage the liminality concept: namely as a celebration of human creativity and freedom, connected to normlessness and going-beyond existing traditions that bound and limit human beings. In short, Turner’s own identification of his project as a postmodern liberation from modern conditions is not a position that should be endorsed. Turner’s notion of the liminoid is not an analytic step forward; quite the contrary. The notion of the liminoid has allowed for an indiscriminate application of liminality. On that account we ought to be slightly conservative and return to the starting point of van Gennep’s analysis: liminality has to do with transition, the ritual forms such transitions take, and the way in which transitions shape both persons and communities. If it is not about transition, it simply is not about liminality. This, however, does not mean that all transitions go smoothly. They in fact do not. Put differently, Turner’s work is crucial, but it needs to be differently grounded in order to sustain its theoretical potential. This is not done by pulling Turner’s 6 And it is not difficult to say when: such single moments can be explosive and life- altering when we already find ourselves in a liminal moment, open (fragile, receptive, vulnerable) to external input. 7 The book entitled Political Anthropology, having Turner as one of the editors, was clearly an embryonic attempt to inject a processual approach into the study of politics, but the liminal nature of modern politics was not even hinted at here (see Swartz, Turner and Tuden 1966). Liminality and the Modern 86 terminology back into a Durkheimian framework – as infelicitously suggested by Rothenbühler (1988). Turner himself (not to mention van Gennep!) would have revolted at seeing his work caged by the -isms he had so carefully and explicitly liberated himself from. Rather, in order to open up Turner’s work we must think it through from within, and start by singling out the somewhat overlooked and not always clearly expressed political aspects of his work; and then integrate those dimensions with parallel or complementary efforts to capture social and political processes of change and transition. This will be the focus of the chapter that follows. But this effort must take into account Turner’s late encounter with philosophy, and especially, with the work of Wilhelm Dilthey. Let us briefly consider this encounter before we move on. Turner Encounters Dilthey: The Tying Together of Thought and Experience in Liminality For Turner, the study of liminality was a study of human experience. In his late career, this brought him into contact not only with novel empirical phenomena, but also with a philosophical dimension. He was no expert, but he jumped into it, trying to make his own contribution. Like almost any other brilliant thinker, Turner came to take a strong interest in etymology. In a posthumously published 1980 conference presentation, Turner ventures into the etymology of experience through the PIE (proto-indo-European) root *per (Turner 1985: 226). Etymology is not a study of dead letters, standing in contrast to what van Gennep called ‘living facts’. Etymology actually assumes its full importance exactly within a processual approach. This is because words are not simply ‘derived’ from earlier usages that have been handed over and/or changed over time. Words are themselves condensed symbolic forms, derived from real human experiences; in fact, most of our crucial terms and words developed in what Koselleck has called sattelzeit periods, in liminal periods. This was what van Gennep had intuited from a very early age, and that is why he linked the notion of ‘marks’ with an attempt to revise existing theories of language. Sometimes things happen for which we have no proper words: and that is when we start looking for such words. Words are, in a certain sense, distilled symbolic forms that take shape during a period of transition, in which meanings become ‘fluid’ and therefore open to elaboration. A detailed analysis of the meanings derived from the PIE root *per confirms Turner’s view that experience is intimately tied to liminality and the passing of a threshold. We can here again rely on the summary provided by Szakolczai (2009: 149–50), building on Turner’s own reflection but adding further detail. Turner stresses that the primary meaning of *per is not simply passage, but successful completion of a passage. *Per derivatives also capture the intense emotion that accompanies such attempts, as indicated by terms like ‘fear’ or ‘peril’. A successful passage also assumes a particular ordering in which somebody goes ahead, showing the way or blazing the trail so that others can follow, ‘imitating’ Liminality Rediscovered: With Victor Turner and Beyond 87 him, which corresponds to the need for masters
